speaker: interviewer
text: Number five. Today's date is December 12. I'm interviewing Erna Hilfstein. [CHUCKLES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Hilfstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you were finishing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well I realized that the girls with all those cigarettes, everything, that they were actually prostituting themselves. And this, when I came back, [SIGHS] I said that this is absolutely dreadful. Now the girls, of course, were-- after concentration camp, I couldn't really blame them. But what the English did was really disgusting. And also, when, for example, we wanted to go to a movie, you could not go to a movie unless you went with English soldier. But I wanted to see the movies. I didn't see one in ages, so we were asking with Lucy, some soldier, can we come in with you. And as soon as we're coming to the movie, we're disappearing and going to different direction. But, I considered this. And all this thing was also going on on Americans, except over there they called it also French way, Promenade Chocolate, they call it [NON-ENGLISH] also. And I hated this because it was really completely degrading. Now I found out, before I went to look for my mother, that my husband was alive. And so I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: One man came in, and he had a list of the hundred people who are in [NON-ENGLISH], and my husband was one of them. I had no idea that was French zone. So I went through hell till I came over there, but I found him. And, incidentally, I also found Schindler there. He lived in the same hotel than my husband. It was called Hotel [NON-ENGLISH]. And he lived there with his wife and his girlfriend. Menage a trois. We became again very friendly. And then I said, after I had recovered, because I hitchhiked from northern Germany to Southern, and it was really something. I said, I'm going now with my husband to find my mother. And then Schindler said, I also want to go. He thought that they going to dine him and wine him in Poland, and a few other people also went with us. So we went, a large group, and went also.

speaker: interviewer
text: On train and walking and whatever, until we came to Austria to Linz. And when we came over there, the place where the displaced person were, a lot of them were people from Brunnlitz who knew Schindler. And they called him by his first name-- hi, Oskar. We so happy. And said, where you going? He said, I'm going to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, don't you dare. The minute you going to enter Cracow, there are people who are taken of the list, and they are gunning for you. They are going to arrest you and hand you before you can say, boo. So he went back to [NON-ENGLISH], and we went to Poland. I found my mother. But Poland was different. It was under Russian sort of occupation. It was-- communist Poland was-- and my mother said, after all, you have bit of family in United States. I had two aunts and an uncle and cousins, whatever. My mother gave me the old address. She said, go back to Germany. And Max also had family, very close family. So we-- got ourselves-- you couldn't go out from-- you could go in Poland, but not out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother bought papers that we are Germans who are being exportated, whatever was German. That was in Poland. We came to Pratt, and they discovered that there were false papers. They were returning us to Poland, but we managed to escape. And through the help of some Czech people, strangers, we managed to get through the border and go to Regensburg, and from over there, back to [INAUDIBLE]. So they were going to send you back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Poland. But before we would go to Poland, we would stay at least six weeks in some special camp, which was not the best thing for women. So I said to my husband, well, we are going to run away. Which we managed to do, and we came back to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: We simply start running toward a little forest. And we hid behind some bushes. And they were pursuing us. And when they came to the bushes, they were about to see us, then there was cow over there in the field, and she started mooing. They looked the other direction. And so they skip us, and then we went, and we were going for about two days in the forest, and we didn't have food. We already left all our clothing over there, and then we heard some voices, and there were some Polish people also trying to go to the border. And they said, let's go together. We know that there is a Czech guy over here who work for the railroad company. And he said, he not going to help us, but he might help you-- namely me-- when he sees a young girl asking him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went over there, and the guy said, I will help you. And he dressed me as a-- a peasant woman. He gave me some eggs, and this, put us on train. And he put my husband and the other guy in various bathrooms over there, toilets. And so we went through the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once we went through the border, that was OK. And so we were back in Germany. And then I wrote letters to the Red Cross, and Max also, and found our families. And his uncle immediately-- and his aunt-- send us affidavit. That was '46, but you couldn't come out until '49, because all the Germans would come, and we were Polish citizens, so were in the Polish quarter, which was very small. Finally, we went to a-- a place that was called [GERMAN] in Germany. And I said to the French, listen, this is ridiculous. We have the papers. And for such a long time, so we managed to come, but my mother had papers, different one, and our name was H, and hers was K, so she couldn't go with us. So I said, I'm not going. So my mother said, once you are in the United States, if something happen to me, you can come back. But if you going to stay here, I might go, and you are going to wait another two or three years. So we came. And when I came here, I found my family, which I didn't like too much. Because when I told them I want to go to college, they said, you greenhorn. And they were laughing at me. I went to all the committees which were helping refugees, and I said, help me to go to college. Need a little money, and when I finish college, I will pay off. They wouldn't give me anything, so I got myself a job. And at night, I was going to college. And I-- first, of course, I got my high school diploma. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have children yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my son. And I couldn't do anything until my mother came. And when she came, she stayed seven months in the hospital. She had gallstone surgery. She was very ill. So after she recovered, I went to college. To high school, and then college at night first, and then daytime. And then, of course, I had a regular job, and also I was working on a very important project with Polish Academy of Sciences for 18 and a half years. And I published many books. I became very famous among historians of science. And so I did what I wanted to accomplish, except the hard way, and it took me much longer, because I had to help my husband to support us, and I also-- My son was very brilliant, and he went-- when he was very young boy, he went already to Julliard. He was very talented. And, you know, we wanted to give our children everything what we missed because of the war, so. But I managed. And if I would have gone like my mother wanted me, to business, I would also be a very wealthy woman, but I didn't want to. I wanted to do the thing which-- my mother wanted me first to be a doctor. I said, after all the dead bodies, no way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, be an accountant like I am, or a lawyer. I said no. I don't like it. I started like this. I didn't like it. So I finally did what I wanted to do with my life, and I managed. Are you involved in any Jewish activities now? Any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we belong-- my husband was one of the founders of the new Cracow Friendship Society. And this society not only helps people who are less fortunate, we sending a lot of money for older people in Cracow. We also are very big on Israel. We buy bonds and we vote for Yad Vashem. And we help Israel any way we can because we realize this is our country now. We have something-- in case something happened, we have place to go. We didn't have that when the Second World War started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any Zionist activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are members of Zionist organization, but I was too busy with my stuff for that, but my husband was very, very active. He still is. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so this is it. He also is a-- but he will tell you about it. He is President of the Jewish Center of [INAUDIBLE] already for more than 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you would like to have on tape for people to hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I like very much the slogan, 'never again.' But it is absolutely empty and meaningless. We know now that there are anti-Semites. When I went to the opening of Holocaust Museum, I saw big groups were standing over there with the posters, saying that there was no Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we have to be watchful, and we have to try to persuade the world that killing people is not really a human thing, although we are doing it from immemorial times. And I abhor violence, and I am physically ill when I hear whats going on; assassination, and killing, and terrorism. It-- it bothers me more than I even can express. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Leon Kluger. And the picture was taken in 1923 in Cracow, Poland. I received this from my father's sister, here in the United States, because I didn't have any picture of my father. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the picture of my father and my mother, which I also received here. My mother's name is Anna Shornstein Kluger, and father Leon Kluger. The picture was taken about February 1923. A year and a half before I was born. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the house in which I was born. It belonged to my grandfather. And I took this picture while I was in Poland. The house was much nicer. They added something on the roof which is not in the style of the house. It was a very beautiful house. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is a house in Cracow, where I lived after we moved from grandfather's house. It's [INAUDIBLE] 25, right across from Jagiellonian University. We lived on the first floor. In the same house, but in the back, lived my uncle. And that's also where Schindler lived when he first came to Cracow. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Those are the pictures of my uncle, my mother's brother, and his wife. And I gathered the pictures when I came after the war to Cracow, and I went to make picture [INAUDIBLE] and the photographer had pictures which he took just before the war and during the war. This was the ID cards for them. My uncle's name is Maurycy Shornstein, and this is his wife, Bertha. Her maiden name was Reich. R-E-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Living or dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: They both died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know where their transport went, from city called Bochnia. And had-- uncle lived outside of ghetto, and they took him. Then they were searching for him and his wife, and they couldn't find them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those are my two little cousins, Meritta and Lenora Shornstein. They're children of Uncle Maurycy and his wife, Bertha. This was taken just before the war. Meritta is eight, and Lenora is about two years old. I got it from the same photographer which-- from whom I obtained the other pictures. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my husband and myself at the time when we were honored by the Israeli [INAUDIBLE] Committee. They gave us also this beautiful picture that time. That was two years ago. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was taken a few years ago during a memorial service which we always held around the time when we went from ghetto to concentration camp. And here is I, with the candle, and my daughter, and my older granddaughter with her. We always light the six candles for people, for each million who didn't survive.
